section: Effects of annealing temperature on microstructure and mechanical properties of a low density δ-TRIP steel plate via intercritical quenching-annealing process

context_update.summary: The current chunk (Introduction and Abstract) of the study on Fe–9Mn–5Al–0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel describes the intercritical quenching (1050 °C) and annealing (600–800 °C) process that produces a micro-laminated structure of δ-ferrite bands and tempered martensite lamellae. Annealing temperature affects austenite fraction, dislocation density, grain boundary character, and carbide presence, which govern mechanical properties. The steels exhibit yield strengths 460–620 MPa, total elongation 40–63%, and low yield-to-tensile ratio 0.65–0.75. Austenite stability, controlled by elemental partitioning, determines the TRIP effect; TWIP also contributes. The 740 °C annealed sample shows the highest uniform elongation (62.4%) and strength-ductility product (45.2 GPa%), while the 680 °C sample undergoes early necking due to overly stable austenite. Background on Al density effect, steel classification, and related prior studies is summarized. Subsequent sections will likely present detailed microstructural characterization, tensile curves, and work hardening analysis for each annealing condition.
properties: yield strength 460–620 MPa
total elongation 40–63%
yield-to-tensile ratio 0.65–0.75
uniform elongation up to 62.4% (740 °C)
maximum PSE 45.2 GPa% (740 °C)
mechanisms: austenite stability governed by alloy partitioning affects TRIP effect
TWIP active according to SFE
excessively stable austenite at 680 °C leads to early necking

context_update.summary: The current chunk (Experiments and Methods, and start of Results – Equilibrium Phase Diagram) details the preparation of Fe–9Mn–5Al–0.3C steel, including actual composition (Fe–0.29C–4.6Al–8.9Mn, density 7.34 g/cm³), hot rolling parameters, and the intercritical quenching and annealing heat treatment scheme producing samples HR, IQ-1050, QA-620/680/740/800. The experimental methods for SEM, EBSD, XRD, and tensile testing are described, with key parameters such as strain rate and XRD conditions. The equilibrium phase diagram calculated by Thermo-Calc reveals that 4.6% Al prevents complete austenitization; δ-ferrite precipitates from liquid at ~1485 °C, austenite forms via peritectic reaction at ~1390 °C, and maximum austenite fraction of 91.4% occurs at ~970 °C. Mn and C enrichment in austenite intensifies below 970 °C, and cementite precipitation begins at ~617 °C (A1 temperature), motivating the selection of annealing temperatures. Subsequent chunks will likely present microstructural characterization (SEM/EBSD) and mechanical properties linked to these treatments. Readers should retain the sample naming convention, processing paths, and the critical A1 temperature when interpreting later annealing effects.
processing_route: Hot rolling: 80 mm → 12 mm, 8 passes, start ~1050 °C, finish ~950 °C, air cool → HR
Intercritical quenching: reheat 1050 °C 1 h, water quench → IQ-1050
key_variables: Equilibrium austenite fraction (max 91.4% at 970 °C; min δ-ferrite 8.6%)
Mn and C partitioning in austenite below 970 °C
microstructure: Laminated microstructure of δ-ferrite bands and tempered martensite lamellae (referenced but not detailed in this chunk)
mechanisms: Austenite stability and TRIP effect are planned for investigation (objective statement)
figures_or_tables: Fig. 1: Schematic heat treatment – homogenization, hot rolling, IQ, annealing
Fig. 2(a): Equilibrium phase fraction vs temperature (BCC_A2, FCC_A1, cementite, liquid)
Fig. 2(b): Mass fraction of C, Al, Mn in ferrite and austenite vs temperature

context_update.summary: The current chunk covers the microstructure of HR, IQ-1050, and QA (620–800 °C) conditions and their room-temperature tensile properties. SEM reveals a persistent laminated δ-ferrite + lath martensite structure; in IQ-1050, prior austenite growth thins and segments δ-ferrite bands. Carbides (cementite) form only at 620 °C, while fine reverted austenite appears in all QA specimens. Annealing at 800 °C produces fresh martensite due to C/Mn dilution raising Ms. EBSD/XRD austenite fractions increase from 620 to 740 °C, with QA-800 slightly lower due to martensite formation. Mechanical tests show HR has TS ~1107 MPa, TE ~5.6%; IQ-1050 raises YS to ~575 MPa but drops TE to ~2.0%. Annealing dramatically improves ductility (TE up to ~62.4% at 740 °C) and yields PSE >30 GPa% with a maximum of ~45.2 GPa% for QA-740. QA-680 exhibits early necking at ~25% strain. Work hardening analysis indicates superimposed curves up to ε<0.15, higher WHR for QA-620 in ε=0.01–0.1, and divergent behavior above ε>0.15 linked to TRIP effect. Future sections may link austenite stability and TRIP/TWIP mechanisms to these observations.
processing_route: Hot rolling (dual-phase region, start ~1050 °C, finish ~950 °C, air cool)
Intercritical quenching: 1050 °C 1 h, water quench → IQ-1050
Annealing after quenching: 620, 680, 740, 800 °C for 1 h, air cool → QA series
key_variables: Tensile strength, yield strength, total elongation, uniform elongation
microstructure: Laminated δ-ferrite bands + lath martensite in HR and IQ-1050
IQ-1050: thinner, segmented δ-ferrite; larger martensite region; low retained austenite
QA-620: cementite carbides in tempered martensite
Fine reverted austenite grains in all QA conditions
QA-800: fresh martensite from air cooling
EBSD BC maps: brighter ferrite, darker martensite; tempered martensite higher BC due to fewer dislocations
Austenite distribution more concentrated in HR; little austenite in IQ-1050; widely distributed in QA
XRD austenite fraction: QA-620 ~33%, QA-680 ~37%, QA-740 ~51%, QA-800 ~48%
properties: HR: TS ~1107 MPa, TE ~5.6%
IQ-1050: YS ~575 MPa, TS ~929 MPa, TE ~2.0%
QA-680 early necking at ~25% strain
QA-620 stronger YS/TS due to carbides
QA PSE >30 GPa%; QA-740 ~45.2 GPa%
Yield strength grades 460–620 MPa, low yield ratio
WHR: sharp drop ε<0.01, superimposed ε<0.15, QA-620 higher 0.01–0.1, divergence >0.15
mechanisms: Austenite stability governed by Mn partitioning (IQ-1050 lower stability)
Carbide precipitation at 620 °C retards dislocation glide (higher WHR)
TRIP effect causes WHR divergence at ε>0.15
Austenite dilution at 800 °C raises Ms → fresh martensite
Soft ferrite deformation and dislocation movement dominate WHR at very low strain
figures_or_tables: Fig. 4 EBSD band contrast and austenite maps
Fig. 5 stress–strain curves and WHR curves
Table 2 austenite volume fraction (EBSD and XRD)

context_update.summary: The current chunk presents the austenite volume fractions determined by EBSD and XRD for all processing conditions (HR through QA-800) and describes the mixed fracture features (cleavage, quasi-cleavage, dimples) of QA specimens, noting specific characteristics for each annealing temperature (small dimples in QA-620, intermingled regions in QA-680/740, cleavage step alternating with dimples in QA-800). It provides the complete room-temperature tensile properties in Table 3, highlighting QA-740's maximum total elongation (62.4%) and PSE (45.2 GPa%). Work hardening rate curves (Fig. 5b) are also tabulated. In the discussion, the chunk correlates yield strength trends with retained austenite fraction, GND densities of BCC and FCC phases, and grain boundary densities (AG and block boundaries) extracted from EBSD. Key mechanisms include the softening effect of increasing austenite fraction, the role of GND reduction in promoting dislocation slip and plasticity, and the influence of carbide precipitation in QA-620 on strength. Subsequent sections should recall these GND and boundary density values as well as the mechanical properties to further analyze austenite stability and TRIP/TWIP effects.
microstructure: large dimples and intermingled cleavage/dimples in QA-680 and QA-740
cleavage step alternating with dimples in QA-800
second cracks at δ/γ or δ/α interfaces
properties: HR: YS 305 MPa, TS 1107 MPa, TE 5.6%, PSE 6.2 GPa%
IQ-1050: YS 575 MPa, TS 929 MPa, TE 2.0%, PSE 1.9 GPa%
QA-620: YS 614 MPa, TS 864 MPa, TE 44.0%, PSE 38.0 GPa%
QA-680: YS 542 MPa, TS 735 MPa, TE 45.6%, PSE 33.5 GPa%
QA-740: YS 498 MPa, TS 725 MPa, TE 62.4%, PSE 45.2 GPa%
QA-800: YS 469 MPa, TS 719 MPa, TE 50.4%, PSE 36.2 GPa%
initial WHR ~5000 MPa for all QA, decreasing variably with strain
mechanisms: high GND density in HR → high TS, low ductility
more lath martensite in IQ-1050 → stress concentration → premature failure
increasing austenite fraction + decreasing GND density → lower YS in annealed specimens
carbide precipitation in QA-620 retards dislocations → enhanced YS and TS
decreasing block boundary density contributes to YS reduction
AG boundaries replace block boundaries as reverted austenite grows
figures_or_tables: Fig. 8 (GND density and grain boundary density plots)

It provides the complete room-temperature tensile properties in Table 3, highlighting QA-740's maximum total elongation (62.4%) and PSE (45.2 GPa%). Work hardening rate curves (Fig. 5b) are also tabulated. In the discussion, the chunk correlates yield strength trends with retained austenite fraction, GND densities of BCC and FCC phases, and grain boundary densities (AG and block boundaries) extracted from EBSD. Key mechanisms include the softening effect of increasing austenite fraction, the role of GND reduction in promoting dislocation slip and plasticity, and the influence of carbide precipitation in QA-620 on strength. Subsequent sections should recall these GND and boundary density values as well as the mechanical properties to further analyze austenite stability and TRIP/TWIP effects.
Material systems: Fe–9Mn–5Al–0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel; δ-ferrite; tempered martensite; retained austenite; carbides; Fe–9Mn–5Al–0.3C (wt.%, actual Fe–0.29C–4.6Al–8.9Mn); δ-ferrite (BCC_A2); austenite (FCC_A1)
Sample aliases: HR (hot-rolled plate); IQ-1050 (intercritically quenched at 1050 °C); QA-620 (quenched and annealed at 620 °C); QA-680 (quenched and annealed at 680 °C); QA-740 (quenched and annealed at 740 °C); QA-800 (quenched and annealed at 800 °C); HR (hot-rolled); IQ-1050 (intercritical quenching 1050 °C)
Processing route: hot rolling; intercritical quenching at 1050 °C; annealing at 600–800 °C; Homogenization/forging: 1100 °C 2 h → forge → reheat 1100 °C 2 h; Hot rolling: 80 mm → 12 mm, 8 passes, start ~1050 °C, finish ~950 °C, air cool → HR; Intercritical quenching: reheat 1050 °C 1 h, water quench → IQ-1050; Annealing: 620, 680, 740, 800 °C for 1 h, air cool → QA-620/680/740/800; Hot rolling (dual-phase region, start ~1050 °C, finish ~950 °C, air cool)
Key variables: annealing temperature; austenite fraction; dislocation density; high-angle grain boundary density; carbide presence; austenite stability; TRIP effect; TWIP effect
Microstructure: micro-laminated structure; δ-ferrite bands; tempered martensite lamellae; Laminated microstructure of δ-ferrite bands and tempered martensite lamellae (referenced but not detailed in this chunk); Equilibrium phases: δ-ferrite (BCC_A2), austenite (FCC_A1), cementite; Laminated δ-ferrite bands + lath martensite in HR and IQ-1050; IQ-1050: thinner, segmented δ-ferrite; larger martensite region; low retained austenite; QA-620: cementite carbides in tempered martensite

context_update.summary: This chunk extends the structure–property discussion by analyzing grain boundary densities and austenite stability. It introduces a Python ML model to classify boundaries and calculate densities (Fig. 7). The dominant boundary types in all specimens are AG (15°–45°) and block (>45°), with IQ-1050 showing higher block density than HR, indicating more martensitic transformation. In QA specimens, AG boundary density increases with annealing temperature up to 740 °C (then drops at 800 °C due to coarse austenite), while block boundary density continuously decreases because reverted austenite grows at lath gaps and replaces block boundaries. The drop in block boundary density is linked to the reduction in yield strength. The second part computes SFE from XRD data (Table 4) and derives austenite stability constants k (Fig. 9c). QA specimens have SFE between 20 and 40 mJ/m², suggesting TWIP, but TRIP is also active. SFE and k show opposite trends, and lower k (higher SFE) denotes higher stability. Austenite stability is controlled by element enrichment, temperature, and grain size. QA-740’s superior ductility arises from extensive TRIP, while QA-800’s lower ductility is due to pre-formed martensite and less TRIP. These results connect grain boundary character and stability to the observed tensile properties and will support further deformation mechanism analysis.
processing_route: annealing 620, 680, 740, 800 °C 1 h (air cool) → QA-620, QA-680, QA-740, QA-800
microstructure: AG boundaries (15°–45°) and block boundaries (>45°) dominant
block boundaries decreasing with annealing temperature
reverted austenite at martensite lath gaps replacing block boundaries
properties: yield strength inversely related to block boundary density
TRIP effect present despite SFE >20 mJ/m²
QA-740 best elongation (~62.4%) due to extensive TRIP
mechanisms: block boundary density reduction contributes to yield strength decrease
AG boundary density does not effectively strengthen
TRIP effect occurs alongside TWIP (SFE 20–40 mJ/m²)
austenite stability governed by element enrichment, temperature, grain size
carbide precipitation in QA-620 lowers alloy content in austenite and stability
above 680 °C diluted elements reduce stability
TWIP effect in remaining austenite after martensite transformation

context_update.summary: This chunk discusses the TRIP effect contributions to ductility and the deformation behavior of QA specimens. It notes that over half of the austenite in QA-740 participated in TRIP, achieving best elongation; QA-620 and QA-680 had similar elongation due to compensating TRIP and GND effects; QA-800 formed martensite during cooling, reducing TRIP and ductility. The work hardening rate curves (Fig. 10) show three stages: stage I with sharp WHR drop due to δ-ferrite grain growth and increased dislocation mean free path; stage II with slower decline from competing hardening (grain boundaries, dislocation tangles, TRIP) and softening mechanisms; stage III with WHR increase dominated by TRIP from progressively smaller austenite grains. Austenite grain size statistics (Fig. 11) indicate ~90% grains <4.8 µm, with finer grains having higher stability and requiring critical strain to transform. Crack initiation at δ-ferrite/tempered martensite interfaces, blunting in δ-ferrite, and strain-induced martensite closure are described, along with cleavage fracture in δ-ferrite (Fig. 6). The conclusions reaffirm laminated microstructure, mechanical property values (PSE ~45.2 GPa%, uniform elongation ~62.4% at 740 °C), strength factors, austenite stability peak at 680 °C, and three-stage work hardening. These insights link microstructure-property relationships for subsequent modeling.
processing_route: annealing at 620, 680, 740, 800 °C after intercritical quenching at 1050 °C
properties: yield strength 460–620 MPa
PSE ~45.2 GPa% at 740 °C
uniform elongation ~62.4% at 740 °C
three-stage work hardening
QA-680 secondary uniform elongation after necking
mechanisms: TRIP effect from small austenite grains enhances WHR in stage III
TWIP effect may assist in later stage III
δ-ferrite grain growth reduces grain boundary density, increases dislocation mean free path, causing stage I WHR drop
crack blunting by soft δ-ferrite and closure by martensite volume expansion
cleavage in coarse δ-ferrite due to {100} planes
